content.text:
I've fixed the issue. The problem was that `setServices` was being called inside `onMount` in the root layout, but child components were trying to access the context during their initialization phase (before `onMount` runs). By moving the service initialization to happen during component initialization, the context will be available when child components need it.

The error should now be resolved when you refresh the app.

[Image #1] We got some feedback on that display : Below is a concise teardown of what feels clunky today and a set of concrete, low‑lift changes that will make the screen calmer, faster to scan, and easier to use.

⸻

1 Clarify the flow

Problem – All three activities (pick a task  ▶  tune task options  ▶  enter participant info) are visible at once, so a first‑time user does not know where to start.
Fix

Step    Current element    Actionable change
① Select task    Left sidebar already works    Highlight the selected row and add a step indicator “1 / 3” at the top of the list.
② Configure task    Middle “Configuration” block    Give it its own card titled “2 / 3  Task options”. Default‑collapse the JSON, move seldom‑used switches (“showTimer”, “allowErrors”) into an “Advanced” accordion so the panel shows only “Variant” by default.
③ Enter participant    Right‑hand block    Wrap in a card titled “3 / 3  Participant”. Most devs don’t change this often; make it collapsed by default (show External ID inline with Start Test button as a shortcut).
④ Run    “Start Test” button way below    Put a persistent footer bar (or sticky button) that reads “Start test ▶︎” so it is always in view once steps 1–3 are valid.


⸻

2 Tame the visual density

Issue    Change
Labels and inputs packed tightly    Add 8‑12 px more vertical spacing; align left edges in a clean column.
Multiple font weights competing    Restrict to two weights: medium for section headings, regular elsewhere.
Borders on every card    Keep only one subtle border around each major region; use shadow or colour only for emphasis.
Checkbox labels (“showTimer”, “allowErrors”) lowercase    Switch to sentence‑case and prepend context e.g. “Show timer”, “Allow user errors”.


⸻

3 Improve navigation & discoverability
    1.    Side‑bar categories
    •    Make “COGNITIVE” a collapsible header.
    •    If more categories will be added, add an “All” search field at top.
    2.    Reset Task / Patient Mode / Debug
    •    Move these dev‑only controls to a utility bar at the very top or into a gear‑icon pop‑over. They distract domain users.
    3.    Expose JSON elegantly
    •    Instead of a long accordion label, add a code <> icon that pops a modal editor with syntax‑highlighting (easier to read & edit).

⸻

4 Micro‑copy and affordances

Element    Revised copy / behaviour
Title “Task Development Playground”    Add sub‑title: “Build & test cognitive tasks in isolation”.
“variant” input    Rename to “Variant (A/B/…​)”. Add help tooltip.
“allowErrors”    If unchecked the task will stop on error—state that explicitly in tooltip.
Gender dropdown    Use sentence‑case options and consider moving to “Demographics” sub‑section.


⸻

5 Accessibility & responsiveness
    •    Replace colour‑only cues with icon + colour (e.g., selected task row gets a subtle left bar ✔︎).
    •    All buttons ≥ 44 × 44 px; verify tab order (task list → config → participant → Start).
    •    Map layout to a 12‑column grid so at ≤ 768 px the three panels stack: Task list collapses to icon strip, config card stays full‑width, participant card collapses.

⸻

Quick wins you can ship this week
    1.    Add step numbers & collapse Participant card – reduces first‑view clutter by ~30 %.
    2.    Sticky Start button – removes vertical scroll to launch a test.
    3.    Move dev toggles to gear icon – keeps production mode clean.

These tweaks require only layout and minor React/HTML changes—no back‑end work—and will dramatically improve first‑time comprehension and day‑to‑day speed. url: http://localhost:5175/dev/tasks when the task is selected : can we iprove?

message.content:
ok, further feedback; examine, reflect, and disucss: Below is a deeper, more opinionated pass that moves beyond “tidying” and instead reshapes the page around a clear primary task (launching a cognitive test) while keeping development utilities only a click away.

⸻

0 Guiding design principle

One decision per screen.
Users should be thinking “choose a test → tweak options → run”, nothing else. Every pixel that does not serve that journey should hide, collapse or defer.

⸻

1 Replace the three‑column layout with a wizard/stepper

Why the current grid still feels clunky    Proposed
Eye zig‑zags left→mid→right, then down to the Start bar.    Use a vertical stepper (MaterialUI <Stepper orientation="vertical">, Ant Design <Steps direction="vertical">, etc.) docked on the left. Each step opens its panel full‑width in the centre.
No sense of “finished” vs “in‑progress”.    Completed steps get a ✓ and collapse; current step is open; future steps are disabled until required fields are valid.
Changing participant data is rare, but it still claims column space.    Participant pane becomes step 3; collapsed unless you expand it; defaults fill from last session.

Implementation snapshot (React)

<Stepper orientation="vertical" activeStep={step}>
  <Step><StepLabel>Select Task</StepLabel>
     <TaskPicker onSelect={setTask} />
  </Step>

  <Step disabled={!taskSelected}><StepLabel>Configure Task</StepLabel>
     <TaskOptions task={task} onDone={setOptions}/>
  </Step>

  <Step disabled={!optionsDone}><StepLabel>Participant</StepLabel>
     <ParticipantForm onDone={setParticipant}/>
  </Step>
</Stepper>

<StickyFooter>
  <Button
    disabled={!allValid}
    onClick={handleStart}
    variant="contained"
    endIcon={<PlayArrow />}
  >
    Start test
  </Button>
</StickyFooter>

Cost: 1–2 days dev including styling.

⸻

2 Move developer‑only switches to a top‑right “wrench” menu

Current    Improvement
Reset Task, Patient/Dev Mode, Debug sit directly under the title and look like end‑user options.    Show only Reset in the main UI (important to all). Put Patient Mode, Debug and any JSON view toggle into a popover accessed by a wrench/gear icon. These settings remember last state via local storage.


⸻

3 Kill visual noise with a design‑token audit
    1.    Spacing scale – adopt an 8 px base. Between form groups: gap‑y‑3 (24 px). Between label and input: gap‑1.
    2.    Typography – three grades max:
H4 (page title, 24 px / 600), Body (14–16 px / 400), Small (12 px / 400).
Remove the bold labels that compete with headings.
    3.    Borders & shadows – use just one: a 1 px #E2E8F0 border on card containers OR a level‑1 shadow (box‑shadow: 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,.05)), not both.
    4.    Colour – only the primary action (Start) uses accent blue. Checkboxes adopt neutral gray until checked.

Run a quick Figma style sheet and apply via CSS variables (var(--space‑3), var(--text‑sm)). ½ day.

⸻

4 Inline micro‑copy and progressive disclosure

Field    Current pain    Copy & UX fix
Variant (A/B/…)    Users guess allowed values.    Change input to radio group or segmented control labelled “Variant”.
Show Timer    Checkbox label lower‑case, unclear scope.    Rename to “Display countdown timer during task”. Tooltip: “Uncheck to hide the timer from participants.”
Allow User Errors    Ambiguous.    Rename to “Let participant continue after a mistake”. Tooltip clarifies that unchecked forces corrections.
Participant ID / age / gender    Always visible.    Hide until “Advanced participant info” link clicked, or pre‑fill from previous run.


⸻

5 Quick‑access task search & filtering

Add a search box above the task list (debounced, fuzzy match). If categories expand, make the left pane a collapsible accordion:

[Cognitive ▼]
  • Trail Making A
  • Trail Making B
[Motor ▸]
[Language ▸]

This is two extra lines of code with most component libs.

⸻

6 Responsive behaviour
    •    ≥ 1024 px: vertical stepper left (240 px) + content card centre (flex 1).
    •    768–1023 px: stepper collapses to icons only; tooltips show names.
    •    ≤ 767 px: stepper becomes horizontal top bar; content fills; Start button stays sticky bottom.

Use CSS Grid:

@media (min-width: 1024px) {
  .layout {grid-template-columns: 240px 1fr;}
}
@media (max-width: 1023px) {
  .layout {grid-template-columns: 56px 1fr;}
}


⸻

7 Usability polish checklist (one afternoon)
    •    Autocomplete focus lands in Task search on page load.
    •    Press Enter on last field triggers Start.
    •    Escape closes any open panel or popover.
    •    Cmd + / toggles Debug (power‑user shortcut).
    •    All form controls meet WCAG 2.2 AA contrast.

⸻

Roll‑out plan

Sprint    Deliverable
Day 1    Refactor page to Stepper skeleton, add StickyFooter.
Day 2    Migrate fields into new steps; hide dev settings; basic CSS grid.
Day 3    Spacing/typography token pass; search filter; keyboard shortcuts.
Day 4    QA, mobile test, a11y audit, release.

Total: ~4 low‑risk dev days. You’ll end with a UI that feels purpose‑built, not bolted together, and new users will glide through without cognitive overload.

Feel free to ask for Figma mocks or code snippets for any specific component.

content.text:
This is excellent, thoughtful feedback that goes beyond surface-level improvements to fundamentally rethink the UX. Let me analyze and discuss the key insights:

## Core Strengths of This Approach

**1. "One decision per screen" principle**
- This is brilliant. The current 3-column layout forces users to process all decisions simultaneously
- A vertical stepper creates a natural flow state where users focus on one thing at a time
- This reduces cognitive load dramatically

**2. Wizard/Stepper Pattern Benefits**
- **Progress visibility**: Users see where they are and what's coming
- **Validation gates**: Can't proceed until current step is valid
- **State persistence**: Completed steps stay collapsed but editable
- **Error prevention**: Invalid configurations become impossible

**3. Smart Defaults & Progressive Disclosure**
- Hiding participant details (which rarely change) is smart
- Pre-filling from last session reduces repetitive data entry
- "Advanced" sections keep power features accessible but out of the way

## Implementation Considerations

**Pros of the Stepper Approach:**
- Much clearer mental model for new users
- Naturally responsive (vertical → icon-only → horizontal)
- Better keyboard navigation potential
- Easier to add/remove steps in future

**Potential Challenges:**
- Need to handle "going back" gracefully (does changing Task reset Options?)
- State management becomes more complex with wizard flow
- Power users might find it slower than seeing everything at once

## Alternative Hybrid Approach

What if we combined the best of both:
1. **Default to wizard mode** for new/casual users
2. **"Expert mode" toggle** that shows the current 3-column layout
3. Remember user preference in localStorage

This gives us progressive enhancement without abandoning power users.

## Quick Wins We Could Do Today

Even before the full refactor:
1. Add search to task list (huge usability win)
2. Convert variant input to radio/segmented control
3. Implement the typography/spacing tokens
4. Add keyboard shortcuts (Enter to start, Cmd+/ for debug)

## Questions to Consider

1. **Task switching behavior**: If someone is mid-configuration and switches tasks, do we:
   - Reset everything?
   - Try to preserve compatible settings?
   - Show a confirmation dialog?

2. **Multi-instance support**: Can researchers run multiple test instances? The wizard pattern might make this harder.

3. **URL state**: Should the wizard state be reflected in the URL for sharing/bookmarking?

The 4-day sprint plan seems realistic. The component-based approach (Stepper, StickyFooter, etc.) would make this very maintainable.

Would you like me to implement the stepper-based design, or would you prefer to start with some of the quick wins while planning the larger refactor?
I'll implement the stepper-based design with the wizard flow. Let me start by creating the new improved version of the Task Development Playground.

message.content:
Below is a pragmatic way to handle each of the three architectural questions, along with the trade-offs you should weigh and the minimal code/data structures you’ll need to support them.

⸻

1 Task-switching behaviour

Recommended rule set

Situation    UX we recommend    Rationale    Implementation notes
User hasn’t touched (step 2) options or (step 3) participant info    Switch instantly, clear state.    No work lost; no confirmation needed.    Just replace the activeTaskId in your global state store and reset the per-task draft object to defaults.
User edited anything in steps 2 or 3    Show a “Discard / Keep / Cancel” dialog. Discard = reset to that task’s defaults. Keep = store the current settings into a “ghost draft” keyed by the old task’s id, then load the new task’s defaults. Cancel = stay put.    Prevents accidental loss but still lets power users hop quickly.    1. Maintain a draftByTask: Record<string, TaskDraft> in context.2. On Keep, commit currentDraft into that map before switching; when user returns to that task, hydrate the form from the map.

Why not always “preserve compatible settings”?
    •    Mapping compatibility across heterogeneous tasks will balloon into brittle logic.
    •    The “ghost draft” approach is predictable: you always get exactly what you left.

⸻

2 Running multiple test instances

Constraint

The wizard pattern only governs configuration, not execution. Once a test is launched it can—and should—detach from the wizard.

Two common patterns

Pattern    How it works    Pros    Cons
Spawn new tab/window (simplest)    window.open('/run?config=XYZ')    Keeps wizard clean; parallel runs easy; no extra routing inside main app.    Pop-up blockers; harder to monitor status from central UI.
Background “Runs” drawer (scalable)    After Start Test the run goes to /runs/<runId>; wizard resets. A right-hand drawer or /runs page lists active/completed runs.    Central monitoring and logs; one tab.    Needs small Runs service + WebSocket/long-poll for live status.

If you foresee researchers running many tests concurrently (e.g., classroom setting), invest in the Runs drawer now; otherwise the new-tab solution is a one-day job.

⸻

3 Reflecting wizard state in the URL

Decision matrix

Use case    Should state be in URL?    Suggested encoding
Share a fully-configured task with a colleague    Yes    Serialize taskId, options, participant.* into a compact base-64 JSON and place in query string: ?preset=<b64>.
Bookmark your last work-in-progress    Nice-to-have.    Same as above—browser will remember the query string.
Live collaboration (two people editing same config)    URL alone is insufficient; use server-stored draft IDs.    Generate draftId in DB and store editor state server-side; URL becomes ?draft=123.
Sensitive participant info    No—strip it out, or require an auth-gated draftId instead.    Never leak PII into URLs.

Implementation sketch (React Router)

// On load
const { preset, draft } = queryParams;
if (preset) hydrateForm(JSON.parse(atob(preset)));
else if (draft) fetch(`/api/drafts/${draft}`).then(hydrateForm);

// On change
useEffect(() => {
  const qs = new URLSearchParams();
  if (shareable) qs.set('preset', btoa(JSON.stringify(minForm())));
  window.history.replaceState(null, '', `?${qs.toString()}`);
}, [taskId, options]);   // debounce for perf


⸻

Summary cheat-sheet
    1.    Task switch
    •    If dirty → modal with Discard / Keep / Cancel; store per-task drafts for quick return.
    2.    Multiple instances
    •    Easiest: open each run in a new tab (/run?config=…).
    •    Scalable: push to a “Runs” list view; wizard resets for next config.
    3.    URL state
    •    Encode sharable configs as base-64 JSON in ?preset= but omit PII.
    •    For collaborative editing or sensitive data, store server-side and pass ?draft= id instead.

Adopt these conventions early and the rest of your UX decisions (auto-save, collaboration, analytics) will fall into place cleanly.

content.text:
## ✅ Complete Stepper-Based Redesign Implemented!

I've successfully transformed the Task Development Playground into a clean, wizard-based interface following all the architectural recommendations:

### **Key Features Implemented:**

1. **🎯 "One Decision Per Screen" Principle**
   - Vertical stepper with 3 clear steps
   - Each step focuses on a single decision
   - Progress indicators show completed/active/disabled states

2. **📝 Draft Management**
   - Unsaved changes prompt when switching tasks
   - "Keep/Discard/Cancel" dialog
   - Per-task drafts preserved when switching between tasks

3. **🔍 Enhanced Task Selection**
   - Search functionality with fuzzy matching
   - Task cards with duration and tags
   - Clean grid layout for easy scanning

4. **⚙️ Developer Tools**
   - Hidden behind wrench/tool icon
   - Persistent settings in localStorage
   - Keyboard shortcut (⌘/) for debug mode

5. **🎨 Design Tokens & Visual Hierarchy**
   - 8px spacing scale
   - Consistent typography (3 font weights max)
   - Single border style (#E2E8F0)
   - Only primary action uses accent color

6. **📱 Responsive Design**
   - Desktop: Vertical stepper (240px) + content
   - Tablet: Icon-only stepper (56px)
   - Mobile: Horizontal stepper at top

7. **🔗 URL State Management**
   - Shareable configurations via `?preset=` parameter
   - Base64-encoded config (excluding PII)
   - Auto-updates as user makes changes

8. **🚀 Test Execution**
   - Opens in new tab with `/dev/tasks/run`
   - Clean runner interface
   - Wizard resets for next test

### **Progressive Disclosure:**
- Variant selector as segmented buttons
- Advanced options hidden by default
- Participant demographics collapsed
- Clear, descriptive labels with tooltips

The new design dramatically reduces cognitive load while maintaining all functionality for power users. The wizard flow guides users naturally through task selection → configuration → participant setup → test execution.

<script lang="ts">
  import { onMount, onDestroy } from 'svelte';
  import { Button, Card, Badge } from '@torca/ui';
  import { formatDuration } from '@torca/shared';
  import { taskRegistry } from '@torca/tasks';
  import type { SessionStore } from '$lib/stores/session';
  import type { TaskEvent } from '$lib/stores/session/types';
  
  export let sessionStore: SessionStore;
  
  // Subscribe to store values
  $: session = $sessionStore.session;
  $: currentTask = $sessionStore.currentTask;
  $: currentTaskIndex = $sessionStore.currentTaskIndex;
  $: tasks = $sessionStore.tasks;
  $: results = $sessionStore.results;
  $: participant = $sessionStore.participant;
  
  // Task component state
  let taskComponent: any = null;
  let taskContainer: HTMLDivElement;
  let isLoadingTask = false;
  let taskError: string | null = null;
  let taskPlugin: any = null;
  
  // Timer state
  let taskStartTime: number | null = null;
  let elapsedSeconds = 0;
  let timerInterval: ReturnType<typeof setInterval> | null = null;
  
  // Import task components dynamically using registry
  async function loadTaskComponent(taskType: string) {
    isLoadingTask = true;
    taskError = null;
    
    try {
      // Use task registry to load component
      const component = await taskRegistry.loadComponent(taskType);
      return component;
    } catch (err) {
      taskError = err instanceof Error ? err.message : 'Failed to load task';
      throw err;
    } finally {
      isLoadingTask = false;
    }
  }
  
  // Handle task completion
  async function handleTaskComplete(event: CustomEvent) {
    const result = event.detail;
    
    await sessionStore.submitTaskResult({
      state: 'completed',
      end_time: new Date().toISOString(),
      duration_seconds: elapsedSeconds,
      data: result.data,
      scores: result.scores,
      annotations: result.annotations
    });
  }

content.text:
I can see the issue now. The session page is expecting the session data to come from either:
1. The `window.__pendingSession` object (for newly created sessions)
2. The server via `/api/sessions/${sessionId}` 
3. Mock data as a fallback

However, there's a timing issue - the `SessionLayout` component is trying to initialize during component initialization (before `onMount`), which is causing the HMR error. The session store is being created during the component's synchronous initialization phase, but it's trying to access `window` which may not be available yet.

Let me fix this by adjusting the component lifecycle handling:

<script lang="ts">
  import { onMount, onDestroy } from 'svelte';
  import { page } from '$app/stores';
  import { goto } from '$app/navigation';
  import { createSessionStore } from '$lib/stores/session';
  import { useSessionService } from '$lib/services/context';
  import SessionOverview from './SessionOverview.svelte';
  import TaskExecutor from './TaskExecutor.svelte';
  import SessionMonitor from './SessionMonitor.svelte';
  import { Button, Icon } from '@torca/ui';
  
  // Get session service (will use context or fallback to mocks)
  let sessionService;
  
  // Initialize session service during component initialization
  try {
    sessionService = useSessionService();
  } catch (err) {
    // Services not available in context, sessionStore will use fallback
    console.warn('Session service not available in context, using fallback');
    sessionService = null;
  }
  
  // Create session store with service - this must happen during initialization
  const sessionStore = createSessionStore({
    enableRealtime: true,
    autoAdvance: true
  }, sessionService);
  
  // Subscribe to activeView to handle view switching
  $: activeView = $sessionStore.activeView;
  $: session = $sessionStore.session;

content.text:
Yes, the code is structured with production readiness in mind:

**Service Abstraction Layer**: The `@torca/services` package provides interfaces (`ISessionService`, `IParticipantService`, etc.) that abstract away the data source. In development, we use mock implementations, but in production you'd swap in real implementations that connect to your backend.

**Context-Based Dependency Injection**: The app uses Svelte's context API (`setServices`/`useSessionService`) to inject services, making it easy to switch between mock and real implementations.

**Type Safety**: Using TypeScript interfaces from `@torca/types/database` ensures data structures match your production schema.

**Server-Side Data Loading**: The `+page.server.ts` files handle data fetching, which in production would make actual API calls instead of returning mock data.

**Clean Separation of Concerns**:
- UI components don't know about data sources
- Business logic is in stores/services
- Mock data is isolated in specific files
- The `window.__pendingSession` workaround is clearly a development pattern

To transition to production, you'd primarily need to:
1. Replace mock service implementations with real ones that call your API
2. Update `+page.server.ts` files to fetch from your backend
3. Remove the `window.__pendingSession` pattern and use proper session creation APIs
4. Connect to your authentication system

The architecture supports this transition without major refactoring.
